Software Comparison

Procore vs Fieldwire for Commercial Construction (2026)

Both are used on large commercial jobsites — but they solve different problems. Here's how to decide which one belongs in your commercial project stack.

✓ Updated April 2026

Procore and Fieldwire both appear on commercial construction projects, sometimes on the same project serving different functions. Procore is the enterprise project management backbone — managing contracts, RFIs, submittals, financials, and compliance documentation. Fieldwire is the field operations tool — managing drawings, punch lists, task assignments, and daily inspections at the site level.

The question isn't always "which one is better" — it's often "what does my team actually need, and is one of these tools doing enough of the job that I don't need both?"

Quick comparison

Feature Procore Fieldwire
Contract management ✅ Full ❌
RFI workflows ✅ Full ⚠️ Basic
Submittal management ✅ Full ❌
Drawing management ✅ Strong ✅ Excellent
Field plan markups ✅ Good ✅ Best-in-class
Punch list management ✅ Strong ✅ Excellent
Task management ✅ Good ✅ Excellent
Budget/cost tracking ✅ Full ❌
Offline mobile access ✅ Full ✅ Full
Subcontractor access ✅ Paid seats ✅ Included
Free plan ❌ ✅ Up to 5 users
Starting price Custom (high) Free–$54/user/mo

Where Procore wins for commercial

Enterprise contract and financial management

Procore's contract management, change order workflows, and budget tracking are the most complete in the industry. For commercial GCs managing multi-million-dollar projects with owner contracts, subcontractor agreements, and complex cost structures, Procore provides the financial backbone that Fieldwire simply doesn't offer. RFI and submittal workflows with configurable routing, response tracking, and deadline management are critical on commercial projects — and Procore's are best-in-class.

Owner and stakeholder reporting

Commercial projects typically have owners, owners' representatives, architects, and other stakeholders who need project oversight. Procore's owner access and reporting tools are designed for this — owners can log in to view budget reports, schedule status, and documentation without requiring the GC to generate manual reports. This level of stakeholder transparency is a Procore strength that Fieldwire doesn't match.

Compliance and safety documentation

Commercial projects have significant compliance documentation requirements: insurance certificates, OSHA logs, safety meetings, toolbox talks, incident reports. Procore's safety module handles these in a structured, reportable way. For GCs with OSHA audit exposure or owner-required safety reporting, this depth is important.

Integrations at scale

Procore's marketplace has 400+ integrations, connecting to accounting systems, BIM tools, payroll platforms, and specialty construction software. For enterprise commercial operations that need their PM platform to connect with their broader tech stack, Procore's integration ecosystem is unmatched.

Where Fieldwire wins for commercial

Field-first drawing management

Fieldwire's drawing management and markup tools are better than Procore's for pure field use. The mobile app handles large PDF sets faster, annotation tools are more intuitive, and the offline sync is more reliable in the field. Superintendents and foremen who spend their days with drawings consistently prefer Fieldwire's interface to Procore's for actual plan work.

Task-based field management

Fieldwire's task management is field-optimized: create a task by tapping on a drawing location, assign it to a trade, set a due date, attach photos. The workflow is fast, visual, and accessible to field workers who aren't tech-savvy. Procore's equivalent functionality exists but feels heavier, more suited to project managers behind desks than supers in the field.

Subcontractor accessibility

Procore's subcontractor seat pricing can make project-wide collaboration expensive for large projects with many subs. Fieldwire's pricing model is more accessible for subcontractors, and the free plan supports up to 5 users — meaning small specialty subs can access the platform without additional licensing costs. This matters for commercial GCs who want all subs using the same drawing and task platform.

Speed and simplicity for field operations

Fieldwire loads faster, has a simpler interface, and requires less training than Procore. For field teams where technology adoption is a challenge, Fieldwire's lower friction often results in higher actual usage — which is more valuable than theoretical capability that nobody uses.

Commercial use case: which platform for which role?

Role Recommended Platform Why
Project Executive Procore Financial oversight, owner reporting, contract management
Project Manager Procore RFIs, submittals, budget tracking, schedule integration
Superintendent Fieldwire Daily plan work, field tasks, punch lists, inspections
Foreman Fieldwire Simple, fast field tool — doesn't need Procore's depth
Specialty Sub PM Fieldwire Drawing access and task management without Procore cost
Safety Manager Procore Incident reporting, OSHA logs, observation tracking

Can you use both?

Yes, and many large commercial projects do. Procore manages the project at the contract, financial, and compliance level. Fieldwire serves as the field operations layer — drawings, daily tasks, punch lists. The two platforms have a native integration that syncs drawings and issues between them.

This combination adds cost, but for large commercial projects the two platforms serve genuinely different audiences and solve different problems. The ROI justification is easier when you can clearly separate "Procore for the PM team" from "Fieldwire for the field team."

Who should choose Procore only?

Commercial GCs who want a single platform for both PM and field operations, have budget for enterprise licensing, and are willing to invest in Procore training and adoption across field teams. Procore can do most of what Fieldwire does — the trade-off is usability complexity in the field.

Who should choose Fieldwire only?

Specialty subcontractors and smaller commercial GCs who primarily need drawing management, task tracking, and field inspection tools — without the enterprise financial and contract management features. Fieldwire's price point makes it accessible for companies that can't justify Procore's enterprise pricing.

Bottom line

For commercial project management at the enterprise level, Procore is the clear choice. For field-level operations and drawing management, Fieldwire is the more effective tool. The best commercial projects often use both. Start with Procore if financial and contract management is your top priority; start with Fieldwire if field adoption and drawing management are your primary pain points.

Evaluating both platforms? Autodesk Build is worth adding to your shortlist — it combines strong field tools with enterprise PM capability in a single platform.
